pqs.question: Has the State promulgated primary aviation legislation to enable it to address its obligations?
criteria: Confirm title, date of promulgation and last amendment of all primary aviation legislation.
Verify that the content of the primary aviation legislation is consistent, sufficient (addressing all audit areas as needed) and properly organized.

pqs.question: Does the primary aviation legislation provide for the introduction/adoption and subsequent promulgation of specific operating regulations?
pqs.lead: Verify that provisions allow for the introduction/adoption of specific operating regulations and at least cover:
criteria: All Annexes in the areas of PEL, OPS (including dangerous goods), AIR, AIG, ANS and AGA.

pqs.question: Has the State established a rulemaking process to enable the timely development and promulgation of specific operating regulations in compliance with the Annexes?
criteria: The rulemaking process includes all applicable steps, in conformance with the State’s legal system, as well as realistic timelines for the various steps.
The procedures or other means of compliance require that current specific operating regulations repeal previous specific operating regulations, if required.

All relevant entities (safety oversight entities/investigation authority) are included in the process (during the development and/or consultation) for PEL, OPS (including dangerous goods), AIR, AIG, ANS and AGA.

pqs.question: Has the State established a process for amending its specific operating regulations or, if necessary, its primary aviation legislation?
pqs.note: Implementation of this PQ will be verified in all audit areas.
criteria: Review the established process for evaluating amendments to all Annexes and for determining the need to amend specific operating regulations or primary aviation legislation.
Verify that the process includes all applicable steps and timelines, including the necessary coordination between the relevant technical and legal experts when necessary.

pqs.question: Does the State make the primary aviation legislation, specific operating regulations, directives, orders, circulars, publications, etc. readily available to the public?
pqs.note: The State may make documents available through official gazette, web access, library, etc.
pqs.lead: Review the means for making the following documents available to the public:
criteria: Primary aviation legislation accessibility.
Specific operating regulations accessibility.
Directives, orders, circulars, publications accessibility.

pqs.question: Does the primary aviation legislation provide for the establishment of a civil aviation organization(s)?
pqs.note: This PQ is not linked to the AIG PQ 6.003.
criteria: Review primary aviation legislation for autonomous CAAs or appropriate legal basis in other cases.
Verify the level of authority delegated to the CAA(s).

pqs.question: Does the primary aviation legislation clearly delegate authority to each Director General (DG)/Chief Executive Officer (CEO)?
pqs.note: This PQ is not linked to AIG PQ 6.004.
pqs.lead: Review relevant legislation and verify delegated authority blocks for:
criteria: The legislation stipulates explicit delegated authority for executive functions.
The legislation includes all DGs/CEOs dealing with civil aviation safety oversight.
The authority delegated covers the following audit areas: PEL, OPS (including dangerous goods), AIR, ANS and AGA.

pqs.question: If the State has transferred safety oversight responsibilities to a supranational organization, is there an appropriate legal basis?
criteria: Identify the legal instrument(s) for the transfer.
Confirm notification to ICAO of the transfer of responsibilities.

pqs.question: Does the primary aviation legislation provide for the issuance of additional binding instruments, such as orders and directives?
pqs.note: The primary aviation legislation may also provide for the issuance of non-binding instructions, such as circulars and instructions.
criteria: The inclusion of provisions for the issuance of additional binding instruments in the primary aviation legislation.
The enforcement capability for non-compliance.
The relevant delegation of authority for the issuance of additional binding instruments, including orders and directives.

pqs.question: Has the State established procedures for identifying and notifying to ICAO differences between ICAO SARPs and its legislation?
pqs.note: Implementation of this PQ will be verified in all audit areas.
criteria: The procedures used for the identification and notification of differences, including for specific operating regulations adopted by reference.
The procedures address each entity responsible for the relevant Annex, including AIG, when an entity separate from the CAA has been established.
The procedures include all applicable steps and timelines, including the necessary coordination between the relevant technical and legal experts when necessary.

pqs.question: Does the legislation provide for the granting of exemptions?
pqs.note: The term “exemptions” also includes exceptions, deviations, variations and prolonged extensions. This PQ does not apply to AIG.
criteria: The legislation explicitly provides for the granting of exemptions.

pqs.question: Has the State established a policy and associated procedures for the granting of exemptions?
pqs.note: This PQ does not apply to AIG.
pqs.lead: Verify that the policy and procedures include:
criteria: The criteria for the conduct and review of risk assessments, analyses, aeronautical studies, etc.
The limitations or other conditions imposed, as applicable, when an exemption is granted.
The recording and publication of exemptions granted.

pqs.question: If the State has adopted specific operating regulations from another State, has it established processes for ensuring amendments?
pqs.note: Verify in all audit areas, particularly in AIR regarding national code of airworthiness.
criteria: Determine if the State has adopted specific operating regulations from another State.
Verify that the processes take into consideration the latest amendments to the Annexes to the Chicago Convention, including filing of differences.

pqs.question: Does the primary aviation legislation provide for the enforcement of applicable legislation?
pqs.note: This PQ does not apply to AIG.
criteria: Enforcement provisions are included in the primary aviation legislation.
The legislation specifies clear penalties.
The penalties are sufficient to serve as a deterrent.
The above enforcement scope is in place for PEL, OPS (including dangerous goods), AIR, ANS and AGA.

pqs.question: Does the legislation provide authority for issuance, suspension, revocation and restriction of documents?
pqs.note: The aircraft radio station licence is often issued/suspended/revoked by an entity other than the CAA.
pqs.lead: Verify legal authority for action on the following documents:
criteria: Personnel licences.
Air operator certificates (AOCs).
Certificates of registration.
Certificates of airworthiness.
Aircraft radio station licences.
Aerodrome certificates.
Authorizations, permits and related certificates.

pqs.question: Has the CAA established an enforcement policy and associated procedures?
pqs.note: This PQ does not apply to AIG.
criteria: Verify that the policy and procedures call for enforcement actions that are commensurate with the level/type of infraction or violation identified.
Verify that the procedures address coordination with the CAA’s legal experts when needed.

pqs.question: Does the primary aviation legislation enable civil aviation inspectors to have unrestricted and unlimited access?
pqs.note: This PQ is not linked to AIG PQ 6.021.
pqs.lead: Verify unrestricted inspector access rights to the following areas:
criteria: Inclusion of explicit access provisions in the primary aviation legislation.
Access to Aircraft and flight decks.
Access to Aerodromes and field installations.
Access to Air navigation services facilities.
Access to Hangars, maintenance lines, and approved maintenance organizations (AMOs).
Access to Workshops and technical facilities.
Access to Ramps and operations areas.
Access to Fuel storage and fueling facilities.
Access to Air operator offices and administrative facilities.
Access to Cargo handling areas and storage warehouses.
Access to Aviation training organizations facilities.

pqs.question: Does the primary aviation legislation enable inspectors to access and inspect aviation documents?
pqs.note: This PQ is not linked to AIG PQ 6.023.
criteria: Inclusion of related document access provisions in the primary aviation legislation.
Access to Manuals and corporate logs.
Access to Certificates, approvals, authorizations, permits.
Access to Operating procedures and quality documents.
Access to Technical files, logs and aircraft records.
Access to Personnel files and tracking databases.
Access to Personnel licences and logs.

pqs.question: Does the legislation provide for a clear delegation of sufficient legal authority to civil aviation inspectors?
criteria: Verify provisions in the legislation on delegation of authority to inspectors, either directly or through a sub-delegation framework (e.g., Director General/Chief Executive Officer).
Confirm that inspectors are legally empowered to carry out surveillance activities (e.g., periodic and random inspections, audits, testing, etc.).

pqs.question: Has the State established legislation to empower inspectors to prevent an aircraft from flying?
pqs.note: This PQ applies to OPS and AIR only.
pqs.lead: Verify aircraft grounding laws and timelines:
criteria: Inclusion of explicit grounding provisions in the primary aviation legislation.
The process established, in cases when the inspectors are not empowered to directly prevent an aircraft from flying.
Confirm that the process in place can be executed and applied in a timely manner.

pqs.question: Has the State established legislation to empower inspectors to prohibit any person from exercising the privileges of a license?
pqs.note: This PQ does not apply to AIG.
criteria: Inclusion of privilege prohibition provisions in the primary aviation legislation.
Confirm that the emergency restriction process in place can be applied in a timely manner.

pqs.question: If the State has ratified Article 83 bis, has it modified its primary aviation legislation to recognize transfer protocols?
pqs.note: If State has not ratified Article 83 bis, this PQ is “Not Applicable”.
pqs.lead: Verify validation capabilities under Article 83 bis:
criteria: The primary aviation legislation (or detailed regulations) enables the State to recognize the validity of crew licences, certificates of airworthiness and/or radio station licences issued or rendered valid by the State of the Operator under an Article 83 bis agreement.

pqs.question: Has the State established and implemented a process to ensure the identification and publication in the State’s AIP of significant differences?
pqs.note: Coordinate with all audit areas to ensure that State policy and procedures are implemented.
pqs.lead: Verify process mapping and publishing lines:
criteria: The establishment and effective implementation of procedures and guidelines for identifying significant differences (including coordination with all concerned entities).
The establishment and implementation of procedures for publishing significant differences in the AIP, including coordination with the AIS provider.
